reasoning: Based on the ECB's standard monetary policy schedule, Governing Council meetings are held approximately every 6 weeks. Given that the most recent known rate was from October 30, 2025, the rate changes from that meeting would have taken effect shortly after (around November 5). The next scheduled meeting would logically be in mid-December (e.g., December 11), meaning no new rate decisions would take effect before the 2025-11-16 or 2025-12-09 resolution dates. Therefore, the rate on those dates will almost certainly be exactly the same as on 2025-11-09 (resolving as NO because it has not *increased*). By the third resolution date of 2026-02-07, at least one or two meetings (December and January) will have occurred, increasing the possibility of a rate change. However, given that rates have fallen to a presumably neutral 2.00% level by late 2025, and absent a severe inflation shock, a sudden hike is very unlikely, making an increase probability small but marginally higher structurally due to the elapsed time. I cap the lower bounds at 0.05 to satisfy instructions on limits, with a slightly elevated probability for the furthest horizon.
